m1_arrive.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_arrive.text: Ah. You're early today. Your baba used to come at this time too.

voice_interjections.text: She called him 'your baba' — she knew your grandfather. The grief in her voice isn't about you.

choices.text: You knew my grandfather?
Sorry for the trouble. Here's your tiffin.

m1_knew_baba.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_knew_baba.text: Forty years, your baba brought tiffin to this building. He knew everyone's order by heart. He knew my husband liked extra methi.

choices.text: Your husband — does he still live here?

m1_husband_question.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_husband_question.text: He did. Until three months ago.

voice_interjections.text: Three months. The number people use when it's too new to say 'he passed away' casually but too old to still be crying openly.

choices.text: I'm sorry. I didn't know.

effects.add_note: Husband passed away 3 months ago. She knew Baba.

choices.text: [EMPATHY 2] She needs someone to sit with this, not rush past it.

effects.add_note: Husband passed away 3 months ago. She still sets out his plate.

m1_sorry.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_sorry.text: No, no. How would you know? Come again tomorrow. I'll leave the container outside.

m1_empathy_sit.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_empathy_sit.text: Some days I still put out two cups for tea. Muscle memory, you know? Thirty-two years.

voice_interjections.text: Thirty-two years of the same morning. She's not just grieving a person — she's grieving a whole shape of life.

choices.text: That sounds... right, actually. To remember in your hands.

m1_empathy_end.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_empathy_end.text: Yes. Yes, exactly. Come again tomorrow, beta. I'll have the container ready.

m1_just_tiffin.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m1_just_tiffin.text: No trouble. You're doing your family's work. That's a good thing.

m2_greeting.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_greeting.text: I found some of his old photographs last night. I hadn't opened that box in years.

voice_interjections.text: Old paper and dried marigold. She's been going through his things.

choices.text: How was that?
[EMPATHY 2] Was there one that surprised you?

m2_photos_hard.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_photos_hard.text: Hard. But also — he looked so young in them. I forgot he was ever that young.

choices.text: What was he like?

m2_photos_surprise.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_photos_surprise.text: There was one from before we were married. He's looking at the camera but his mind is somewhere else entirely. I never knew what he was thinking in that photograph. I never asked.

voice_interjections.text: The regret of the unanswered question. The thing you can no longer ask.

choices.text: That sounds like something he left behind for you to wonder about.

effects.add_note: She found an old photo — something in it she never understood. A question she can't ask anymore.

m2_husband_character.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_husband_character.text: He was stubborn. And quiet about the important things. Very quiet. I wonder now if there were things he never said.

voice_interjections.text: She's circling something. This isn't just reminiscing — she's carrying an unasked question.

choices.text: Maybe he said them in other ways.
[GRIEF 1] Maybe some things are said in silences.

m2_end_warm.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_end_warm.text: Maybe. Take the tiffin box back tomorrow when you come. I'll wash it tonight.

m2_end_grief.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m2_end_grief.text: ...Yes. I think you're right. Some things only make sense later, when you're listening differently.

m3_letter.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m3_letter.text: I found a letter today. In his coat pocket — the one he wore to the hospital the last time. He never gave it to me.

voice_interjections.text: She's holding herself very still. This is the thing she's been building toward.
The letter that didn't arrive. The words that never made the crossing.

choices.text: Have you read it?

m3_read_it.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m3_read_it.text: Not yet. I've been carrying it for three days. I don't know if I'm afraid he said something wonderful or something I can't bear to know.

voice_interjections.text: Either way, the letter will change things. She needs to know that's okay.

choices.text: Both things can be true at once.
[EMPATHY 3] Whatever it says — it's from him. That's what matters.

effects.add_note: She found his unsent letter. She hasn't read it yet. Spirit world: the letter waits.

m3_end_empathy.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
m3_end_empathy.text: Yes. Yes. It's from him. Whatever he said — it was him saying it. That's enough.

sn_1.text: A shape stands in the lantern light. She doesn't see you yet. Or perhaps she doesn't want to.

si_1.speaker: NARRATOR
si_1.text: The streets look the same, but the light falls differently here — gold through indigo, everything a little further away. She stands by the window of what used to be Flat 4B. She's holding something.

voice_interjections.text: You recognise this version of her. More transparent. The part that stays up when everything else has gone to sleep.

choices.text: Mrs. Mehta?

si_2.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
si_2.text: Oh. You're one of the ones who can see. Your grandfather could too, you know. He used to bring us what we needed without knowing it.

choices.text: What are you holding?
Did Baba ever have something he couldn't deliver?

si_mehta_baba.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
si_mehta_baba.text: Only once, he told me. He said in forty years he feared just one delivery — his own last one. To you. He never could work out how to hand it over.

voice_interjections.text: He carried the very silence you've spent this whole route teaching other people to break.

choices.text: ...Thank you for telling me.

effects.add_note: Baba feared only one delivery in forty years: his own last one, to me.

si_3.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
si_3.text: His letter. I've been holding it in both worlds. I still can't open it.

voice_interjections.text: She can't do it alone. That's why she's still here.

choices.text: I can stay while you open it.
[GRIEF 2] Some letters need a witness.

si_end.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
si_end.text: Come again tomorrow night. I think I'll be ready then.

si_end_grief.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
si_end_grief.text: A witness. Yes. I never thought of it that way. Come back. Tomorrow night, I'll read it.

sr_1.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
sr_1.text: You came back. Good. I have the letter.

voice_interjections.text: She's not shaking anymore. She's decided.

choices.text: I'm here. Whenever you're ready.

sr_2.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
sr_2.text: 'Rani — I know I am not good at saying these things while you can hear me. So I am saying it now in writing, which is easier for a coward like me. I loved the thirty-two years. Every single one. Don't be so quiet without me.'

voice_interjections.text: Oh. There it is. He knew. He just couldn't say it face to face.

choices.text: He wasn't quiet about the important things after all.

effects.add_note: Letter delivered. He loved the thirty-two years. She heard him, finally.

sr_end.speaker: Spirit — Mrs. Mehta
sr_end.text: No. He wasn't. He just needed someone to carry the words the last few steps. Thank you. Go now. I'm going to sleep.

sa_1.speaker: Mrs. Mehta
sa_1.text: She's standing in the light of the lanterns, and she looks — lighter. As if she set something down.

raju.name: Raju
raju.title: The Boy on the Temple Steps

r1_caught.speaker: Raju
r1_caught.text: I didn't take anything. I was just... smelling it. The tiffin.

voice_interjections.text: He's lying. The tiffin on the bottom of the stack is lighter than it should be. One container is nearly empty.
Dirty sneakers, school bag, uniform three days unwashed. He's not homeless — he's run away.

choices.text: You're hungry. That's all right.
[NOSE 1] I can see the container is empty. Who taught you to eat so quietly?
That tiffin was for Flat 2A.

r1_generous.speaker: Raju
r1_generous.text: I'll pay you back. I have money at home.

voice_interjections.text: He said 'at home' not 'I don't have money.' He has a home. He just isn't in it.

choices.text: Don't worry about it. I'll bring extra tomorrow.

effects.add_note: He's a runaway, not homeless. Has a home. Too proud to say more.

r1_caught_gentle.speaker: Raju
r1_caught_gentle.text: ...My ammi taught me. She said eating quietly was polite. I was trying to put it back before you noticed.

choices.text: Your ammi sounds like a careful person. I'll bring you your own tomorrow.

effects.add_note: His mother taught him to eat quietly. He's been here a few days.

choices.text: Wait — are you all right?

r1_end_warm.speaker: Raju
r1_end_warm.text: I'm fine. I'm just... between things right now.

choices.text: I said I would. How long have you been sleeping here?

r2_how_long.speaker: Raju
r2_how_long.text: Nine days. But I'm not sleeping here. I'm thinking.

voice_interjections.text: That distinction matters to him. He's not running — in his mind, he's making a decision.

choices.text: Nine days is a long time to think.
[STREET_SENSE 2] What are you thinking about?

r2_why_left.speaker: Raju
r2_why_left.text: They sent me to this boarding school. To get a good future. But I was the only boy from here. Nobody talked to me like — like I was from anywhere.

voice_interjections.text: Loneliness as a form of erasure. He left to find himself and ended up more lost.

choices.text: So you left.

effects.add_note: Ran from boarding school. Felt invisible, erased. Has a family waiting.

choices.text: [EMPATHY 2] And now nobody knows where you are.

r2_why_left_direct.speaker: Raju
r2_why_left_direct.text: I'm thinking about whether going home means I lost. Or whether I was allowed to want to come home.

choices.text: Wanting to go home doesn't make you a loser.

effects.add_note: He thinks going home = admitting defeat. Pride vs longing.

r2_end.speaker: Raju
r2_end.text: ...Yeah. I left. I don't know if I want to go back.

r2_end_empathy.speaker: Raju
r2_end_empathy.text: My ammi doesn't know. My baba will be so angry. He saved for two years for that school.

r3_phone.speaker: Raju
r3_phone.text: I borrowed a phone from the chai man. To call home. I stood there for four minutes and then I gave it back.

voice_interjections.text: He dialled and couldn't do it. The pride and the longing are equally strong.
Four minutes is a long time to hold a phone and decide not to speak.

choices.text: What stopped you?

r3_stopped.speaker: Raju
r3_stopped.text: I didn't know what to say after 'hello.' Everything after 'hello' would have to be an explanation.

choices.text: 'Hello' might be enough.
[STREET_SENSE 2] They already know you're coming home. They're just waiting for the call.

effects.add_note: He needs courage to call. The words 'hello' are the whole door.

choices.text: Because you're their son. You could call ten years later and they'd pick up.

voice_interjections.text: He's crying. Very quietly. Like someone who was taught to eat quietly.

sn_1.text: A small shape sits on the spirit-steps. He's holding something small and luminous — you can't tell what it is yet.

si_1.speaker: Spirit — Raju
si_1.text: You can see me here too. I wondered if you would.

choices.text: What are you holding?

si_2.speaker: Spirit — Raju
si_2.text: A phone. But here it doesn't ring. I've been carrying the call I didn't make.

voice_interjections.text: The unmade call is heavier than any call you make. It lives in the gap.

choices.text: Can I deliver it for you?

si_end.speaker: Spirit — Raju
si_end.text: You can't call for me. But maybe you can show me how it goes. Come back tomorrow.

sr_1.speaker: Spirit — Raju
sr_1.text: I'm ready to call. I just need — I need to hear myself do it. Can you be here?

choices.text: I'm here. Say 'hello.'

sr_2.speaker: Spirit — Raju
sr_2.text: ...Hello? Ammi? It's me. ...Yes. Yes, I know. I'm coming home.

voice_interjections.text: There it is. The whole weight of nine days in four words.

choices.text: (Say nothing. Let him have this.)

effects.add_note: He made the call. He's going home.

sr_end.speaker: Spirit — Raju
sr_end.text: The phone vanished. I think — I think it worked. Go. I'm going to go find a real phone now.

sa_1.text: The steps are empty. He's gone home.

d1_start.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_start.text: New delivery person, hah? Your parking permit is not valid for this lane. I can overlook it... for a consideration.

voice_interjections.text: Standard shakedown. But his hands are shaking too. The villain in this scene is tired.
Hospital disinfectant under the sweat. He came from somewhere before work.

choices.text: How much of a consideration?
[NOSE 2] You smell like Nair Hospital. Long morning?
I'll move the bicycle.

d1_bribe.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_bribe.text: One hundred rupees. And you can come and go freely.

choices.text: (Pay it. It's easier.)

effects.add_note: Took a small bribe. Tired, not cruel.

choices.text: I don't have it today. I'll bring it tomorrow.

d1_hospital.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_hospital.text: ...What? I don't— yes. I had an appointment. Never mind that.

voice_interjections.text: He flinched. That question landed somewhere real.

choices.text: I hope it was a good appointment.

effects.add_note: Goes to Nair Hospital. Won't say why. The extortion softened when I mentioned it.

d1_hospital_soft.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_hospital_soft.text: ...Move your bicycle out of Window 3's eyeline. Window 4 is fine. Go.

d1_paid.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_paid.text: Tomorrow, same time, it lapses. Good day.

d1_defer.speaker: Desai Sahib
d1_defer.text: Tomorrow then. Don't make me come looking.

d2_start.speaker: Desai Sahib
d2_start.text: You again. You're consistent. I'll give you that.

voice_interjections.text: Hospital again. More than an appointment. This is routine.

choices.text: I brought you your tiffin. New route — you're on the list now.
[NOSE 2] Nair Hospital again today?

d2_tiffin.speaker: Desai Sahib
d2_tiffin.text: I didn't ask to be on a list.

choices.text: I know. It's a gift. First one's free.

d2_hospital_again.speaker: Desai Sahib
d2_hospital_again.text: My wife. She's been — she has her treatments on Tuesdays and Fridays.

voice_interjections.text: He said it quietly. The way people say things they've had to say too many times to cry over anymore.
He's not angry with you. He's angry with something much larger and taking it out on people at his eye level.

choices.text: I'm sorry. That's a hard schedule to carry.

effects.add_note: Wife is ill. Treatments Tuesdays and Fridays at Nair Hospital. The bribes are funding it.

d2_wife_reveal.speaker: Desai Sahib
d2_wife_reveal.text: It is what it is. Don't — don't tell anyone. This is not your business.

choices.text: It won't leave this lane.

d2_end_trust.speaker: Desai Sahib
d2_end_trust.text: ...Leave the tiffin. And come from Window 4 side tomorrow. I'll tell them you're expected.

d3_confession.speaker: Desai Sahib
d3_confession.text: I've been thinking. What I take from the department — for her treatments. I know what it is. I've known for two years.

voice_interjections.text: He didn't just decide to confess. He needed someone who already knew the right amount — not everything, enough.

choices.text: Why are you telling me this?

d3_why.speaker: Desai Sahib
d3_why.text: Because you're the only person who brings me food and doesn't want something from me. That's — that means something, strangely.

choices.text: Is there another way to fund the treatments?

effects.add_note: He confessed the source of the bribes. Wife's medical costs. Wants a way out.

choices.text: [EMPATHY 3] You've been alone with this for two years.

d3_end.speaker: Desai Sahib
d3_end.text: I'm looking into it. There are government schemes I should have applied for. I was too — too proud. Or too frightened.

d3_end_empathy.speaker: Desai Sahib
d3_end_empathy.text: Two years, four months, and eleven days. I counted.

sn_1.text: A figure in a government uniform stands very still. He's surrounded by floating papers. They won't stop moving.

si_1.speaker: Spirit — Desai Sahib
si_1.text: The papers won't stop. Every one of them is a form I filled wrong. Or filled right for the wrong reason.

choices.text: What would make them stop?

si_2.speaker: Spirit — Desai Sahib
si_2.text: I don't know. The form I actually need to fill — the right one — I can never find it.

voice_interjections.text: He's looking for a legitimate path. One he should have taken. That's what he needs delivered.

choices.text: Maybe I can find it for you.

si_end.speaker: Spirit — Desai Sahib
si_end.text: You would do that. Come back.

choices.text: PM-JAY. National health scheme. She qualifies. You qualify. You always did.

sr_2.speaker: Spirit — Desai Sahib
sr_2.text: ...I knew about PM-JAY. I never applied because applying meant I was admitting I couldn't afford it myself.

voice_interjections.text: Pride as its own kind of poverty. He couldn't bear the paperwork of needing help.

choices.text: Applying means you love her more than you need to seem fine.

effects.add_note: Spirit delivered: the form he never filled. PM-JAY. He needed permission to need help.

sr_end.speaker: Spirit — Desai Sahib
sr_end.text: The papers have stopped. ...I'll fill it in the morning. The real one.

sa_1.text: The papers are gone. He's standing in clear air for the first time in what must feel like years.

a1_new.speaker: Arjun
a1_new.text: New address. I just moved back. My father lives here — I'll be staying for a while.

voice_interjections.text: New to this address, but not new to this city. He knows how to stand on these streets. He grew up here.

choices.text: Welcome back. Two portions?
How long have you been away?

a1_two_portions.speaker: Arjun
a1_two_portions.text: One. He doesn't eat much. And I'm not sure he'll eat at all if I'm the one who ordered it.

voice_interjections.text: Something happened between them. He came back anyway. That means something.

choices.text: I'll bring two anyway. He might surprise you.

effects.add_note: Returned from abroad. Complicated relationship with his father. Father may not accept food.

a1_away.speaker: Arjun
a1_away.text: Eight years. I was in Canada. I came back when my father's health started — well. I came back.

voice_interjections.text: He stopped himself from finishing that sentence. Whatever he came back for, it's complicated by grief.

choices.text: That's a long time. And a long way.

a1_end.speaker: Arjun
a1_end.text: Yes. Well. Leave the tiffin at the door. We'll... sort it out.

a2_start.speaker: Arjun
a2_start.text: He ate from the tiffin. He didn't say anything about it. But he ate.

voice_interjections.text: The container you're collecting smells of the same sabzi you've been sending. He ate exactly his portion and no more. Neither wasteful nor reaching for seconds — the discipline of a man sitting with his pride.

choices.text: That's something.

a2_that_something.speaker: Arjun
a2_that_something.text: My mother used to say that with him. 'It's something. Take it.' She was very good at reading the space between what he said and what he meant.

voice_interjections.text: Past tense. She's gone.

choices.text: Your mother — she passed?

a2_mother.speaker: Arjun
a2_mother.text: Six years ago. I didn't come back for the funeral. I told myself I couldn't afford the flight. That was — it wasn't true.

voice_interjections.text: He's carrying this like a stone he can't put down.

choices.text: Does your father know you wanted to come?

effects.add_note: Wasn't at mother's funeral 6 years ago. His reason was a lie. Father doesn't know he wanted to come.

choices.text: [GRIEF 1] That kind of regret doesn't have a name in English.

a2_father_knows.speaker: Arjun
a2_father_knows.text: No. He thinks I didn't care. I've let him think that for six years.

a2_grief_response.speaker: Arjun
a2_grief_response.text: No. In Hindi either. I've looked.

a3_start.speaker: Arjun
a3_start.text: I tried to talk to him last night. About Amma. He left the room.

choices.text: He's not ready yet?
[EMPATHY 2] Or maybe he's too ready — he's been sitting with it alone for six years.

a3_ready.speaker: Arjun
a3_ready.text: Maybe. Or maybe I said it wrong. I don't know how to talk to him anymore. I forgot the language, somehow.

choices.text: Maybe the language was always actions, not words.

a3_empathy.speaker: Arjun
a3_empathy.text: ...Both of us have been sitting alone with it. That's — I hadn't thought of it that way. He lost her too.

choices.text: The door between you is the same door. Just approach from the other side.

effects.add_note: The door is the same door — father and son both standing at opposite sides.

a3_end.speaker: Arjun
a3_end.text: He used to come fix things in my room without asking. I hated it. Now I'd give anything to hear him puttering around.

a3_end_empathy.speaker: Arjun
a3_end_empathy.text: The same door. ...I'll try again tonight.

sn_1.text: A figure stands before a door that won't open. He's been knocking for a long time.

si_1.speaker: Spirit — Arjun
si_1.text: I've been standing here for six years. The door looks like our house. But it won't open.

choices.text: What would open it?

si_2.speaker: Spirit — Arjun
si_2.text: Saying the thing I never said. But I can't say it until he hears it.

voice_interjections.text: He needs the door to open from the inside. Someone on the other side has to be listening.

choices.text: Say it to me. I'll carry it to him.

si_end.speaker: Spirit — Arjun
si_end.text: Come back. I need a little more time to find the words.

sr_1.speaker: Spirit — Arjun
sr_1.text: I found the words. I want to say them and I want you to deliver them.

choices.text: I'm ready.

sr_2.speaker: Spirit — Arjun
sr_2.text: Tell him: I was a coward, Pitaji. I was afraid you'd see how sorry I was. I came back because I'm done being afraid of you seeing me.

voice_interjections.text: The truth that was too heavy to carry alone. Now it has wheels.

choices.text: (Carry the words to Pitaji's door in the spirit-world.)

sr_3.speaker: NARRATOR
sr_3.text: You walk to the other side of the door. On this side, an old man in the spirit-city. He heard. He was always on the other side, also waiting.

choices.text: (The door opens.)

effects.add_note: The door opened. Both men were waiting on opposite sides.

sa_1.text: The door stands open. Both of them are inside.

champa.name: Champa Tai
champa.title: The Tea Stall at the Corner

c1_intro.speaker: Champa Tai
c1_intro.text: Arre, you're Babulal's grandchild! You look just like him. Sit, sit — have chai first, tiffin second.

voice_interjections.text: Cardamom, ginger, and two types of tea leaf. She doesn't cut corners. This stall has standards.

choices.text: You knew my grandfather?
I shouldn't stop — I have deliveries.

c1_grandpa.speaker: Champa Tai
c1_grandpa.text: Twenty-five years I've had this stall. He stopped here every morning. Never took sugar. Said it confused the taste.

choices.text: That sounds like him.

effects.add_note: 25 years at this stall. Knew Baba well. He took chai without sugar.

c1_babulal_seed.speaker: Champa Tai
c1_babulal_seed.text: That last month, before he passed — he kept saying he had 'one more delivery' to make. Wouldn't tell me to whom. Stubborn old goat. I think, beta, he meant you.

voice_interjections.text: One more delivery. He never made it. That's why the route came to you unfinished.

choices.text: To me?

effects.add_note: Champa: Baba spoke of 'one more delivery' in his last month. He never made it.

c1_busy.speaker: Champa Tai
c1_busy.text: Five minutes. The chai is already poured. Your grandfather never said no to chai.

choices.text: (Sit down.)

c1_end_warm.speaker: Champa Tai
c1_end_warm.text: Come every day. I know everyone's business and I'll tell you none of it. But I'll give you the right chai for whatever mood you're in.

c2_homesick.speaker: Champa Tai
c2_homesick.text: Baba — sorry, I keep calling you that — something in your chai today? It tastes different to me.

voice_interjections.text: She's not talking about the chai. Something in her mood changed the flavour of the whole interaction.

choices.text: It tastes the same to me. Why different to you?
[NOSE 2] It's not the chai. What's different today?

c2_why_diff.speaker: Champa Tai
c2_why_diff.text: My sister called from the village this morning. My mother's mango tree finally died. It was forty years old.

voice_interjections.text: Forty years. The tree she grew up with. The one whose shade held her whole childhood.

choices.text: Did you plant it?

c2_nose_direct.speaker: Champa Tai
c2_nose_direct.text: Ayo, clever one. My mother's mango tree. Forty years. Gone this morning.

choices.text: Forty years is a long life for a tree.

c2_tree.speaker: Champa Tai
c2_tree.text: My father planted it the year I was born. I climbed it every summer. The last time I was home was fourteen years ago.

voice_interjections.text: The tree is a calendar of everything she hasn't gone back to.

choices.text: Do you miss home?

effects.add_note: Hasn't been home in 14 years. Mother's mango tree died. Her village is a ghost she carries.

c2_miss_home.speaker: Champa Tai
c2_miss_home.text: I made this city my home. That was the deal I made with myself when I came at twenty-two. But sometimes... sometimes the old home doesn't care about your deal.

c3_train.speaker: Champa Tai
c3_train.text: I bought a train ticket last week. To go home. I've been carrying it in my apron every day. I haven't booked the time off.

voice_interjections.text: The ticket is a promise she made to herself. The time-off is the part that makes it real.

choices.text: What's stopping you from booking it?

c3_fear.speaker: Champa Tai
c3_fear.text: If I go home, I might not want to come back. And this stall — these people — this is also my home now. I don't know how to hold both.

voice_interjections.text: The terror of the divided self. She belongs to both places and fully to neither.

choices.text: You won't have to choose. Both can be real.

effects.add_note: Holding a train ticket. Afraid that going home means leaving here. Needs permission to hold both.

choices.text: [EMPATHY 2] This place became home because of what you put into it. That won't disappear if you leave for two weeks.

c3_end.speaker: Champa Tai
c3_end.text: Both can be real. I'll think about that.

c3_end_empathy.speaker: Champa Tai
c3_end_empathy.text: Twenty-five years I've been here. The regulars know my schedule. The stall knows my hands. You're right — it won't forget me in two weeks.

sn_1.text: The tea stall glows in the lantern-light. A woman stands between two directions, as if she can't remember which way she's facing.

si_1.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
si_1.text: Ah. The delivery boy who sees. Sit. I don't have real chai here but sit anyway.

choices.text: You're between two places here too.

si_2.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
si_2.text: I'm always between two places. Here in this city-dream, I can see both at once. The mango tree. This stall corner. They're the same distance.

voice_interjections.text: She's closer to peace than she thinks. She just needs someone to witness the distance and say it's okay.

choices.text: What do you need delivered?

si_end.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
si_end.text: Permission. I need someone to tell me I'm allowed to be from two places. Come back, beta.

sr_1.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
sr_1.text: You came back. You have it?

choices.text: You are allowed to be from two places. Both are real. Both are yours.

sr_2.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
sr_2.text: Both are mine. ...Yes. Yes, that's right. The mango tree is dead but I remember it and that means it's still mine. And this corner is mine. And I can go home and come back and it will all still be mine.

voice_interjections.text: Grief that finds its shape. Not an ending — a belonging that spans distance.

choices.text: Go home, Champa Tai. We'll hold the stall.

effects.add_note: Delivered permission to belong to both places. She's going home.

sr_end.speaker: Spirit — Champa Tai
sr_end.text: Ha! You have no idea how to run a stall. But yes. I'm going. I'll bring back good mangoes.

sa_1.text: The stall is still there, glowing warmly. A handwritten note: 'Back in two weeks. Kettle is on the left.'

babulal.name: Babulal
babulal.title: The Route That Was Left to You

b1.speaker: NARRATOR
b1.text: Five spirits are at rest. The lantern-city is going dark, one flame at a time — all but the last, burning at the corner where your route begins each morning. Someone waits beneath it. You know the walk before you see the face.

voice_interjections.text: You have carried everyone's last words but one. You always knew whose they were.

choices.text: ...Baba?

b2.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b2.text: So. You can see them now. I spent your whole childhood hoping you never would. And a small, selfish part of me hoping you would.

voice_interjections.text: He is not surprised to see you. He has been standing at this corner the whole time.

choices.text: You left me the route.
[GRIEF 2] You had one delivery left. To me.

b3.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b3.text: Forty years I ran this route. I knew every order, every grief on every staircase. But there was one tin I never set down — the thing I meant to say to you.

choices.text: Say it now. I'll carry it the last few steps for you.

b3_grief.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b3_grief.text: You've grown a nose for the unsaid — I can tell. Yes. My own last delivery. I carried it so long it went cold in my hands, and still I couldn't hand it over.

voice_interjections.text: The deliveryman who could route anything, undone by the one address that was his own kitchen.

choices.text: Then let me be your witness. Say it.

b4.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b4.text: I was proud. Too proud to be a burden while I was leaving. So I taught you the route instead of teaching you goodbye. Cowardice, dressed up as a gift.

voice_interjections.text: Every person on your route was some version of this man. You have been practising for this conversation all along.

choices.text: The route WAS a gift. I understand it now.
[EMPATHY 3] You didn't have to say goodbye. You just had to let me carry you the last few steps.

b5_warm.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b5_warm.text: Then take the last words on my route, beta. I saw you. Every single day of you. Not once was I disappointed. Now put the tin down. Go home and eat something warm.

choices.text: (Take the words. Hold them level, the way he taught you.)

b5_empathy.speaker: Spirit — Babulal
b5_empathy.text: ...You always understood the work better than I did. The last words, then — and they are simple. I saw you. All of you. I was proud every single day. Now set the tin down, and let me set myself down too.

voice_interjections.text: This is what you have been learning to do on every staircase: let someone put a heavy thing down at last.

choices.text: (Take the words. Hold them level, the way he taught you.)

b6.speaker: NARRATOR
b6.text: The last lantern doesn't blow out — it is set down, gently, the way you lower a tiffin you've carried a long way. The street is only a street again. Somewhere ahead of you, morning is already starting to warm the stone.
